Clarify limits on agentic AI data access to not exceed patient-authorized access.
Maintain standardization and reporting requirements to ensure safe AI development.
Provide targeted support to smaller and newer developers rather than removing requirements.
notable_quote: Removing privacy and security criteria transfers the onus to healthcare organizations lacking security infrastructure.

summary: MLCommons supports the HTI-5 proposed deregulatory actions, emphasizing that interoperability via FHIR APIs is foundational for AI-enabled healthcare and advancing the Quintuple Aim. They advocate for federated evaluation approaches to preserve data privacy while enabling robust AI assessment and urge that deregulation preserve safety guardrails for AI systems.
key_arguments: FHIR APIs enable scalable AI integration and population health analytics supporting the Quintuple Aim.
Federated evaluation platforms like MedPerf allow robust AI assessment while preserving privacy and reducing duplicative validation costs.
Open, neutral benchmarking builds clinician and patient trust and reduces costs.
Deregulation should not compromise AI safety and effectiveness, especially for high-risk clinical applications.
recommendations: Maintain robust mechanisms for evaluating AI safety and effectiveness despite deregulation.
Support federated evaluation and benchmarking through cooperative agreements and recognition of community standards.
Continue advancing FHIR-based interoperability to enable AI integration and data sharing.
Ensure technical policies provide transparency and integrity for AI governance.
notable_quote: Interoperability is foundational for AI and the Quintuple Aim: The proposed FHIR-forward approach directly enables AI evaluation, benchmarking, and deployment at scale.

summary: SNOMED International supports deregulatory efforts that reduce burden on health IT developers but cautions against removing key standards that ensure patient safety and semantic interoperability. They emphasize the critical role of SNOMED CT in maintaining data quality and recommend its continued use and explicit referencing in future standards and criteria.
key_arguments: Removing certification criteria may reduce incentives for developers to update to latest SNOMED CT versions, risking data quality.
FHIR provides transport but SNOMED CT ensures clinical data integrity and semantic interoperability.
Removal of Implantable Device List criterion risks losing critical device attribute data encoded with SNOMED CT, impacting patient safety.
Continued referencing of SNOMED CT prevents semantic fragmentation and regression to proprietary local codes.
recommendations: Continue referencing SNOMED CT in remaining and future certification criteria and voluntary standards like USCDI.
Explicitly list SNOMED CT as preferred terminology in any criteria converted to functional-only requirements.
Retain SNOMED CT as the backbone terminology to avoid costly adoption of new terminologies like ICD-11.
Caution against removal of Implantable Device List criterion to preserve clinical context and patient safety.
notable_quote: We caution the agency to ensure that patient safety, trust, and semantic interoperability are not compromised by the removal of key guardrails.

summary: The commenter urges protection of the human right to healthcare for all Americans and immigrants, emphasizing the importance of supporting vulnerable populations. The comment implies concern about potential negative impacts of deregulation on healthcare access and equity.
key_arguments: Healthcare is a human right that must be protected for all individuals.
The strength of the nation depends on protecting its most vulnerable populations.
Deregulatory actions may undermine equitable access to healthcare.
recommendations: Ensure that any regulatory changes do not compromise healthcare access or equity.
Protect vulnerable populations in the implementation of health IT policies.
notable_quote: Please protect the human right to healthcare for all Americans and those who choose to come to the country.

summary: The commenter emphasizes the need to accelerate adoption of the latest FHIR Implementation Guides (IGs) across US states and territories to achieve nationwide Medicaid interoperability. They urge CMS and ASTP/ONC to monitor and report on the adoption status of key FHIR IGs such as Da Vinci, CARIN, and Gravity Project.
key_arguments: Many US states and territories have not yet adopted the latest FHIR IGs at scale.
Acceleration is needed to align all states and territories with current FHIR standards.
CMS and ASTP/ONC should actively monitor and report on FHIR IG adoption progress.
Tracking adoption of specific IGs (Da Vinci, CARIN, Gravity) is critical for interoperability.
recommendations: Accelerate adoption of Da Vinci, CARIN, and Gravity FHIR IGs in all US states and territories.
CMS and ASTP/ONC should monitor and publicly report adoption metrics for these IGs.
notable_quote: CMS and ASTP/ONC need to monitor FHIR IG adoption by US states and territories.

commenter_name: South Carolina Primary Health Care Association
organization: South Carolina Primary Health Care Association
summary: The South Carolina Primary Health Care Association supports certain interoperability improvements in the HTI-5 proposed rule but strongly opposes the removal of key privacy and security certification criteria. They emphasize that deregulatory actions should not weaken safety and security safeguards critical for safety net providers like CHCs, which face workforce and financial constraints. They urge ONC to retain audit log requirements and ensure protections that support vulnerable populations and maintain trust in health IT.
key_arguments: Removing privacy and security certification shifts costly and complex burdens to resource-constrained CHCs, risking security flaws and liability exposure.
Strong federal health IT standards are essential for safe, coordinated, and equitable care delivery in underserved, rural, and high-need communities.
Workforce shortages and financial instability limit CHCs' ability to absorb new technical and compliance responsibilities without harming patient care.
Supporting interoperability improvements like removing write-back restrictions and recognizing AI agents will reduce administrative burden and improve care coordination.
recommendations: Retain audit log certification requirements (§ 170.315(d)(2), (3), and (10)) to detect fraud and abuse.
Maintain privacy and security certification criteria to ensure vendor software supports HIPAA compliance.
Provide sufficient transition time, targeted funding, and clear accountability for certified developers to avoid imposing undue burdens on CHCs.
Continue engagement with safety net providers to ensure final policies are workable and protect vulnerable populations.
notable_quote: Removing federal 'floors' for certification will shift the burden of verification from the government to resource-constrained health centers.

summary: The Physical Activity Alliance urges ONC to adopt USCDI v4 instead of v3.1 to include physical activity assessment in certified health IT, aligning with CMS and CMMI policies and leveraging existing FHIR implementation guides to improve interoperability and support population health.
key_arguments: Inclusion of physical activity assessment in USCDI v4 enables standardized capture and exchange of physical activity data in certified EHRs.
Adoption aligns with CMS and CMMI policies promoting prevention and lifestyle-based care.
Existing HL7 FHIR Physical Activity Implementation Guide supports integration and interoperability.
Physical activity data is critical for chronic disease prevention, improved health outcomes, and cost containment.
recommendations: Adopt USCDI v4 in §170.213 instead of USCDI v3.1.
Support certification pathways that enable standardized physical activity data exchange.
Consider incentivizing adoption of the Physical Activity Implementation Guide within certification criteria.
notable_quote: Adoption of USCDI v4 is essential to enable standardized capture, exchange, and use of physical activity data within certified health IT.

summary: J Michael Consulting supports a FHIR-enabled future for health data interoperability but urges retention of critical certification requirements, especially for data security and public health reporting. They caution against removing CDA-based document exchange requirements prematurely and emphasize the need for consistent standards to avoid interoperability issues.
specific_provisions: Transmission to Cancer Registries certification requirement
key_arguments: FHIR is key to future interoperability but CDA-based documents remain critical today for patient care and public health.
Removing certification criteria without clear standards risks variable implementations and burdens on public health and private sectors.
Security and patient data protection must remain paramount in certification requirements.
Transition from CDA to FHIR-based reporting requires realistic timelines and resource support for public health agencies.
recommendations: Retain CDA-based document exchange certification criteria until FHIR-based alternatives are fully supported and resourced.
Maintain existing security requirements until better privacy and security standards are developed.
Ensure certified HIT products maintain a consistent baseline functionality to support interoperability and a level playing field.
Incorporate requirements for FHIR Subscription and Bulk Data Access with cohort creation capabilities in certified HIT.
notable_quote: We strongly recommend that Certification Criteria retain requirements that certified HIT adhere to existing HL7 interoperability standards.

summary: The College of American Pathologists supports the overall goals of the HTI-5 proposed rule to reduce burden and promote interoperability but raises concerns about removing AI transparency requirements and the immediate release of pathology results to patients without clinician context. They emphasize patient safety and trust in AI, and urge exceptions to information blocking rules to allow clinician review before releasing complex lab results.
key_arguments: Maintaining AI transparency requirements is essential for pathologists to assess AI tools, ensure patient safety, and build trust.
Removing these AI informational requirements risks increased waste, fraud, and abuse in healthcare.
Immediate release of pathology and lab results without clinician interpretation can cause patient confusion, distress, and hinder care coordination.
A blanket exception to the Information Blocking Rule is needed to allow clinician review and contextualization before releasing complex test results.
recommendations: Retain the informational requirements for AI decision support interventions in the Health IT Certification Program.
Establish a blanket exception to the Information Blocking Rule for pathology and laboratory results to allow clinician review prior to patient release.
notable_quote: The CAP urges a blanket exception to allow clinicians to provide medical context before releasing pathology results.

summary: The commenter, an attorney experienced in reviewing electronic medical records, opposes the proposed removal or reduction of certification criteria related to audit logs and EHI export. He stresses that deregulation should not occur before modernizing audit trail standards to ensure patient safety, record integrity, and enforceable patient access.
key_arguments: Audit trails are essential for transparency, accountability, and patient safety by tracking edits and modifications.
Removing certification criteria without updated standards risks undermining the accuracy and reliability of medical records.
Patients face barriers to accessing complete records; regulatory reform should reinforce patient access rights.
Emerging technologies influencing care should be included in audit logs to maintain traceability.
recommendations: Prioritize modernization of audit trail standards before reducing certification requirements.
Ensure audit logs capture clinically significant actions, user roles, and access activity.
Include logging of AI systems, connected devices, and messaging platforms affecting patient care.
Preserve clinically relevant communications in durable, readable formats consistent with retention laws.
Reinforce enforceable patient access rights under the 21st Century Cures Act.
notable_quote: Patients rely on their records as a source of truth. Federal health IT policy should ensure those records remain transparent, durable, and trustworthy.

summary: The American Academy of Family Physicians supports ONC's intent to reduce health IT certification burdens to spur innovation and reduce costs, especially for small practices. However, they caution against removing foundational interoperability standards like C-CDA and clinical reconciliation requirements prematurely, emphasizing the need for a deliberate transition to FHIR-based APIs with consistent implementation and business incentives.
specific_provisions: Health IT Certification Program criteria removal and revision
key_arguments: Reducing certification burden may encourage new health IT developers and benefit small practices.
C-CDA remains a critical baseline for care coordination and should not be removed before viable FHIR alternatives are mature.
Standardized APIs alone are insufficient without consistent implementation practices and business incentives.
Removing clinical reconciliation certification prematurely risks patient safety and increases physician burden.
recommendations: Align ONC and CMS certification criteria and timelines to reduce regulatory confusion.
Retain and revise clinical reconciliation certification to support FHIR and expand data elements rather than remove it outright.
Establish best practices for CEHRT developers to ensure predictable, efficient API integration, potentially making them mandatory over time.
Avoid removing C-CDA certification requirements until proven FHIR-based replacements are universally implemented.
notable_quote: Removing C-CDA-related certification risks destabilizing the data exchange floor before viable alternatives exist.

summary: The American College of Radiology supports transparency in AI decision support interventions and requests alternative transparency mechanisms if model card requirements are removed. They support the withdrawn requestor preferences exception with modifications and seek clarity on automated access to EHI to ensure compliance with privacy laws.
key_arguments: Algorithm transparency is important for qualified end-users to safely implement predictive DSIs.
If model card requirements are removed, alternative transparency mechanisms should be implemented.
Requestor preferences exception provides useful flexibility for ethical sharing of test results.
Automation technologies accessing EHI must operate on behalf of lawful requestors under HIPAA or other laws.
recommendations: Implement alternative transparency mechanisms coordinated with FDA if model card requirements are removed.
Codify the requestor preferences exception with modifications to reflect routine patient preference collection.
Clarify that automated technologies accessing EHI must act only on behalf of lawful requestors.
notable_quote: ACR generally supports algorithm transparency to ensure qualified end-users can identify and implement predictive DSI.

summary: The North American Association of Central Cancer Registries supports HTI-5's goals to reduce burden and modernize standards but opposes removing the §170.315(f)(4) certification criterion for cancer registry transmission before FHIR-based reporting is mature. They warn this removal risks disrupting timely, standardized cancer reporting, increasing provider burden and reducing data quality.
key_arguments: FHIR-based resources for cancer registry reporting are immature and not widely implemented.
Removing §170.315(f)(4) risks shifting reporting to manual or non-standardized processes, increasing burden and reducing data quality.
Certification criteria incentivize EHR support for interoperability and standardized cancer data reporting.
Without a certified national standard, jurisdictions face increased workload and resource demands.
recommendations: Retain §170.315(f)(4) certification criterion until FHIR-based reporting matures.
Implement a phased transition plan with defined minimum content expectations and coordinated testing to prevent reporting gaps.
notable_quote: Removal of §170.315(f)(4) risks disrupting timely incidence cancer reporting.

commenter_name: Community Health Center Association of Connecticut
organization: Community Health Center Association of Connecticut
summary: The Community Health Center Association of Connecticut opposes the removal of federal privacy and security certification criteria in the HTI-5 proposed rule, citing increased operational risk and cybersecurity vulnerabilities for safety net providers with limited IT resources. They support interoperability improvements like easing information blocking and AI system access but caution that deregulatory actions should not weaken essential safeguards relied upon by community health centers.
key_arguments: Removing certification criteria shifts compliance and risk management burdens onto resource-constrained health centers lacking control over software design.
Certification safeguards are critical for HIPAA Security Rule compliance and protecting sensitive patient data in complex, multi-vendor environments.
Workforce shortages and limited in-house cybersecurity expertise make vendor-based certification essential for operational and financial risk mitigation.
Interoperability improvements like removing write-back restrictions and recognizing AI systems as users will reduce administrative burden and improve care coordination.
recommendations: Retain federal privacy and security certification criteria to maintain minimum safety and security standards.
Provide sufficient transition time, targeted funding, and clear accountability for certified developers to support health centers.
Continue to support interoperability enhancements such as removing the 'Third Party Seeking Modification Use' condition and clarifying AI system access.
Avoid deregulatory actions that could lead to unbundling of standard features into costly add-ons or removal of essential functionalities.
notable_quote: Removal of certification 'floors' materially increases operational risk, financial exposure, and vulnerability to cybersecurity threats.

summary: The commenter, an individual attorney, strongly opposes the HTI-5 proposed deregulatory actions that would remove or weaken audit trail and privacy/security certification criteria. He argues that audit trails are essential for patient safety, data integrity, and accountability, and urges ONC to retain these safeguards until modernized standards are established.
key_arguments: Audit trails are foundational for trust, transparency, and accountability in electronic health information.
Removing audit requirements reduces the ability to detect errors, prevent harm, and investigate fraud.
Deregulation should not precede the establishment of updated standards for data creation, preservation, and exchange.
Weakening privacy and security safeguards shifts risk onto patients and erodes public confidence.
recommendations: Retain and reinforce audit trail certification criteria until modernized standards are codified.
Update definitions and standards for audit logs, tamper resistance, and traceability to ensure consistent expectations.
Do not remove privacy and security safeguards until robust mechanisms protect data integrity and patient access.
Avoid deregulation that undermines tools for identifying systemic risk and protecting patients.
notable_quote: An audit trail is not an optional feature. It is the foundation of trust in electronic health information.

summary: The commenter emphasizes the critical importance of data retention and preservation standards, opposing the removal of key certification criteria related to audit logs and data reproducibility. They urge HHS to prohibit ephemeral messaging and modernize data preservation standards before any deregulation occurs to ensure patient safety and prevent fraud.
key_arguments: Accurate data retention is essential for patient care, investigations, and legal compliance.
Ephemeral messaging or automatic data deletion must be prohibited to preserve data integrity.
Data preservation standards must be modernized and enforced before deregulating certification.
Removing audit trail requirements risks patient safety and increases vulnerability to fraud.
recommendations: Prohibit use of ephemeral messaging or systems allowing automatic data deletion.
Retain existing certification criteria in sections 170.315(d)(10), (d)(2), (d)(3), (b)(11), and (g)(3).
Develop clear, uniform definitions for ePHI, EHI, Generative AI, and Synthetic Data.
Modernize, codify, and enforce data preservation and reproducibility standards before deregulation.
notable_quote: We strongly urge HHS to prohibit the use of “ephemeral messaging” or any system that permits automatic deletion of data.

summary: Family Caregiver Alliance supports the FHIR-first certification reset but urges inclusion of standardized caregiver data elements. They oppose removal of AI model card transparency requirements and support information blocking reforms with caregiver protections, emphasizing the need for caregiver data infrastructure in health IT.
key_arguments: Certified EHRs currently lack standardized caregiver data elements, limiting interoperability for caregiver-inclusive care.
Removing AI transparency requirements risks harm to vulnerable populations relying on predictive decision support.
HTI-5's FHIR-first reset is an opportunity to embed caregiver data as core infrastructure, not an afterthought.
Caregiver data exchange supports cost avoidance in Medicaid HCBS programs and aligns with state and federal policies.
recommendations: Include caregiver identification, strain assessments, and dyadic care planning data elements explicitly in FHIR API scope and future USCDI versions.
Maintain AI model card transparency requirements for predictive tools used in vulnerable populations.
Add protections for caregiver-inclusive care coordination data in information blocking reforms.
Recognize caregiver outcomes as qualifying metrics in AI reimbursement and patient safety frameworks.
notable_quote: Without this inclusion, the FHIR-first certification reset will leave America’s 53 million family caregivers invisible.

summary: Y Combinator strongly supports the proposed rule's information blocking reforms, urging removal of specific infeasibility exception conditions and contract fairness provisions to enhance startup access to EHR data. They highlight how incumbent EHR vendors exploit current exceptions to limit competition and innovation, harming patients and providers.
specific_provisions: § 171.204(a)(3) Third Party Seeking Modification Use Condition
key_arguments: Incumbent EHR vendors exploit exceptions to block startup access to EHR data, suppressing innovation.
Removing § 171.204(a)(3) is essential to enable two-way data exchange critical for clinical AI and care coordination.
The Manner Exception Exhausted Condition (§ 171.204(a)(4)) should be removed entirely to prevent manipulation by incumbents.
Contract fairness provisions are necessary to address power imbalances and prevent unconscionable terms and excessive fees.
recommendations: Finalize the strongest versions of information blocking reforms, especially removing §§ 171.204(a)(3) and (a)(4).
Adopt the contract fairness provisions in § 171.301(a)(2)(iii) to prohibit contracts of adhesion, unconscionable terms, and above-market pricing.
Explicitly include automated access (AI and robotic process automation) in definitions of access and use.
Maintain Security and Preventing Harm Exceptions for genuine security concerns.
notable_quote: Classic monopoly playbook: dominant vendor making things much harder for healthcare software companies.

summary: The Oncology Nursing Society supports removing the 'Transmission to Cancer Registries' certification criterion to reduce burden, recognizing ongoing modernization efforts toward FHIR-based reporting. However, they urge delaying removal until cancer registries can practically receive electronic data to avoid disrupting cancer surveillance.
key_arguments: The current CDA-based criterion is duplicative as cancer registry reporting evolves toward FHIR.
Cancer case reporting remains mandated by state laws, so removal won't affect legal obligations.
Removing the criterion prematurely risks shifting burden to manual processes and harms data timeliness and completeness.
Modernized cancer registry reporting initiatives (e.g., USCDI+ Cancer) are underway but not yet fully ready.
recommendations: Delay removal of the Transmission to Cancer Registries certification criterion until central cancer registries demonstrate readiness for modernized electronic reporting.
Ensure removal does not disrupt cancer surveillance data collection.
notable_quote: ONS urges ASTP/ONC to delay removal until registries demonstrate readiness to support modernized electronic reporting.

summary: AHIMA supports some deregulatory efforts in HTI-5 but strongly cautions against removing key certification criteria such as C-CDA, patient matching, decision support interventions, and privacy/security requirements. They emphasize the risk to nationwide interoperability, patient safety, and data privacy if these provisions are removed prematurely.
specific_provisions: 45 CFR § 170.315(d) Privacy and Security Certification Criteria
45 CFR § 170.550(h) Privacy and Security Certification Framework
key_arguments: Removing C-CDA certification criteria risks disrupting nationwide interoperability and limits data exchange for under-resourced providers.
Eliminating patient matching requirements jeopardizes patient safety by increasing misidentification risks.
Removing the Decision Support Interventions criterion reduces transparency and trust in AI/DSI tools, hindering innovation adoption.
Dropping privacy and security certification criteria exposes sensitive patient data amid rising cyber threats and increases burden on providers.
recommendations: Retain C-CDA certification criteria until FHIR is fully developed, tested, and widely adopted.
Maintain patient matching requirements or adopt provisions from HR 2002 and the MATCH IT Act to improve patient matching standards.
Preserve the Decision Support Interventions criterion and explore ways to improve it rather than removing it.
Do not remove privacy and security certification criteria; seek alternative reforms through future rulemaking.
notable_quote: Removing C-CDA functionality without understanding readiness jeopardizes ongoing nationwide interoperability.

commenter_name: North American Association of Central Cancer Registries
organization: North American Association of Central Cancer Registries
summary: The North American Association of Central Cancer Registries supports HTI-5's goals to reduce burden and modernize standards but opposes removing the §170.315(f)(4) certification criterion for cancer registry transmission before FHIR-based reporting is mature. They warn this removal risks disrupting timely, standardized cancer reporting, increasing provider burden, and reducing data quality.
key_arguments: FHIR-based resources for cancer registry reporting are immature and not widely implemented.
Removing the certification criterion risks shifting to manual or non-standardized reporting, increasing burden and reducing data quality.
Certification incentives are critical to ensure EHR support for standardized cancer data reporting, especially in outpatient settings.
Transitioning without a phased plan will increase technical complexity and risk reporting gaps.
recommendations: Retain §170.315(f)(4) certification criterion until FHIR-based reporting matures.
Implement a phased transition plan with defined minimum content and coordinated testing to avoid reporting gaps.
notable_quote: Removal of §170.315(f)(4) risks disrupting timely incidence cancer reporting and increasing provider burden.

summary: Drummond Group supports the overall direction of HTI-5 to streamline certification and focus on FHIR-based interoperability but urges a more cautious, phased approach to removing many certification criteria. They emphasize the need to maintain foundational privacy, security, and clinical workflow assurances, especially during early AI adoption, and recommend aligning and consolidating overlapping API requirements to reduce confusion and burden.
key_arguments: Removing large clusters of certification criteria risks gaps in assurance and increases variability across developers.
Source attribute requirements for AI-enabled decision support are critical for transparency and clinician trust during early adoption.
Privacy and security criteria removal may leave smaller providers vulnerable without a baseline assurance mechanism.
Aligning and consolidating overlapping API requirements (e.g., DSI API and FHIR API) would reduce developer confusion and burden.
recommendations: Adopt a phased, targeted approach to removing certification criteria with clear replacement assurance mechanisms.
Retain foundational privacy and security criteria or require developer attestation to recognized security frameworks.
Align (b)(11) DSI API data support requirements with (g)(10) FHIR API or consider eliminating redundant requirements.
Retain some source attribute requirements for predictive decision support to ensure transparency and explainability.
Coordinate certification streamlining with other HTI-5 changes to avoid undermining safety or interoperability.
notable_quote: Removing large clusters of criteria at once risks unintended gaps in baseline assurance and increased variability.

summary: Myriddian LLC cautions against removing the current cancer reporting certification criteria before FHIR resources are fully validated and widely adopted. They emphasize that premature removal could disrupt cancer surveillance workflows and negatively impact data timeliness and accuracy.
specific_provisions: 170.315(f)(4) certification criterion transmission to cancer registries
key_arguments: Current FHIR resources for cancer reporting lack sufficient validation and real-world testing.
Removing established certification criteria prematurely would disrupt cancer surveillance workflows.
Disruptions would impair continuity, completeness, and timeliness of cancer case reporting.
Timely and accurate cancer data is essential for monitoring trends, disparities, research, and policy.
recommendations: Maintain existing certification criteria for cancer reporting until FHIR resources are broadly tested and stable.
Ensure sufficient adoption across vendors and healthcare systems before deregulating.
notable_quote: Removing certification criteria before FHIR readiness would significantly disrupt cancer surveillance workflows.

summary: The commenter urges acceleration of FHIR API adoption and alignment with the latest FHIR Implementation Guides (IGs) across US states and territories to promote nationwide Medicaid interoperability. They recommend CMS and ASTP/ONC monitor adoption rates of specific FHIR IGs to track progress.
key_arguments: Many US states and territories have yet to adopt the latest FHIR IGs at scale.
Acceleration and alignment with Da Vinci, CARIN, and Gravity Project IGs are critical for nationwide interoperability.
CMS and ASTP/ONC should actively monitor adoption progress across states and territories.
recommendations: Accelerate adoption of FHIR APIs and latest FHIR IGs in remaining US states and territories.
CMS and ASTP/ONC should track and report on adoption metrics for key FHIR IGs such as Da Vinci PDex, CDex, PCT, CARIN digital insurance cards, and Gravity SDOH Clinical Care.
notable_quote: CMS and ASTP/ONC need to monitor FHIR IG adoption by US states and territories.

summary: Mosaic Life Tech supports ONC's deregulatory and FHIR-forward goals but raises concerns about removing AI model card requirements, highlighting a governance readiness gap for autonomous AI access to EHI. They urge ONC to maintain organizational accountability and provide guidance and support to under-resourced providers to ensure patient safety and transparency.
specific_provisions: definitions of 'access' and 'use' including autonomous AI systems
information blocking exceptions (Infeasibility Exception, Manner Exception)
key_arguments: Removing AI model card requirements creates a transparency gap shifting burden to health systems unprepared for AI governance.
Safety-Enhanced Design is important for AI-enabled clinical decision support and should be modernized, not fully removed.
Expansion of 'access' and 'use' definitions to include autonomous AI systems introduces new governance challenges requiring guidance.
Disparities exist between well-resourced and under-resourced providers in AI governance capacity, risking widened inequities.
recommendations: Publish non-binding guidance on reasonable organizational AI transparency practices based on HTI-1 framework.
Coordinate with The Joint Commission to align accreditation expectations with federal policy on AI transparency.
Establish a voluntary disclosure framework for developers maintaining model card-equivalent documentation.
Consider technical assistance programs similar to Regional Extension Centers to build AI governance capacity in under-resourced organizations.
Refer safety validation for AI-enabled health IT to HITAC for development of a modernized, less burdensome approach.
Clarify guidance on managing autonomous AI-mediated access under tightened information blocking exceptions, especially the Manner Exception.
notable_quote: Removing these requirements without a replacement mechanism creates a transparency gap that shifts the entire burden of AI tool evaluation to individual health systems.

summary: Aquila Health supports the HTI-5 deregulatory efforts to reduce regulatory burden and accelerate innovation but urges a shift from prescriptive compliance to measurable performance standards. They emphasize the need for governance safeguards, AI transparency, federated trust architectures, and prioritization of data quality to ensure interoperability modernization is effective and trustworthy.
key_arguments: Documentation-based certification does not guarantee real-world interoperability performance.
AI readiness requires governance frameworks addressing provenance, auditability, and reidentification risks.
Federated architectures better support scalable, secure, and accountable data sharing than centralized models.
Data quality and semantic standardization are foundational to effective interoperability.
recommendations: Replace checklist-based certification with outcome-based interoperability validation.
Recognize federated trust architectures as preferred modernization pathways.
Clarify governance safeguards as permissible under information blocking rules.
Integrate AI transparency and auditability requirements alongside deregulation.
Elevate data quality measurement and improvement as a national interoperability priority.
notable_quote: Durable innovation fundamentally requires durable trust. HTI-5 is poised to advance both objectives.

summary: AdvaMed supports streamlining outdated certification criteria and modernizing interoperability with FHIR-based APIs to reduce burdens and foster innovation. However, they caution ONC to consider the continued need for non-FHIR standards, clarify AI-related information blocking definitions, and ensure technical and safety distinctions justify differentiated access under the Manner Exception.
specific_provisions: definitions of 'access' and 'use' including AI systems
key_arguments: Non-FHIR standards like HL7 v2 and Direct-based exchange remain essential for imaging and clinical workflows and should not be deprecated.
Treating all automated AI data access as equivalent to human access may create compliance ambiguity and burdens.
Technical, safety, and regulatory differences justify differentiated access and should be recognized in the Manner Exception.
Reducing unnecessary compliance burdens can accelerate innovation and deployment of advanced medical technologies.
recommendations: Clarify that removal of certification criteria does not imply federal preference to deprecate non-FHIR standards.
Provide additional guidance on actor responsibilities and compliance in multi-system AI environments.
Confirm FDA-regulated AI device developers are not subject to duplicative oversight under information blocking rules.
Clarify that technical, safety, and regulatory distinctions are legitimate grounds for differentiated access under the Manner Exception.
notable_quote: Reducing unnecessary compliance burdens can create capacity for innovation and accelerate deployment of advanced medical technologies.

summary: The commenter emphasizes the critical need to protect patient information and maintain the integrity of medical records by strengthening audit trail and preservation standards before any deregulation. They stress that patients must have confidence in the accuracy of their records and transparency about who accesses them and why.
key_arguments: Patients must trust that their medical records are accurate and complete.
Access to patient records should be transparent, including reasons and actions taken.
Current audit trail and preservation standards need strengthening before deregulation.
Strong rules are essential to maintain integrity and patient trust.
recommendations: Update and strengthen rules and regulations regarding audit trails and preservation standards before implementing any deregulation.
notable_quote: A patient must have confidence that what is contained in their medical record is a true and accurate record.

summary: The Minnesota e-Health Initiative supports interoperability and burden reduction but strongly opposes deregulatory actions that remove AI transparency, privacy, security, and audit log requirements, citing risks to patient safety, privacy, and equity. They urge maintaining key certification criteria and pairing policy changes with funding and support for under-resourced settings.
specific_provisions: removal of AI model card and transparency requirements
removal of privacy and security certification criteria
removal or reduction of audit log requirements
proposed adoption of USCDI v3.1 with removal of key demographic elements
removal of C-CDA and Direct certification criteria without FHIR readiness
removal of public health-related certification criteria (e.g., cancer registry transmission)
revisions to information blocking definitions and exceptions
key_arguments: Removing transparency and risk management shifts responsibility from vendors to providers who lack resources to evaluate AI tools safely.
Eliminating privacy and security certification risks widening digital trust gaps and leaves providers without necessary vendor capabilities.
Removing audit log requirements could leave providers legally vulnerable without technical support from health IT products.
Lack of demographic data in USCDI v3.1 and removal of public health criteria may worsen health disparities and impair public health functions.
recommendations: Maintain AI transparency and risk management certification criteria including model cards.
Retain privacy and security certification requirements to ensure baseline protections.
Keep audit log related certification criteria to support HIPAA compliance.
Ensure USCDI includes key demographic data elements to support equity and public health.
Delay removal of C-CDA and Direct criteria until widespread FHIR readiness is achieved.
Pair deregulatory policy changes with funding and implementation support for under-resourced providers.
notable_quote: The proposed deregulatory changes risk shifting responsibility for AI safety, clinical decision support reliability, cybersecurity, and legal accountability from regulated health IT developers to provider organizations.

summary: MedStar Health urges ONC to retain usability testing requirements to prevent design flaws that could cause patient harm and clinician inefficiency. They emphasize the importance of maintaining safeguards to ensure safety and usability in health IT products.
key_arguments: Poor design can lead to deadly mistakes
Usability testing prevents daily frustration and inefficiency
Safeguards are essential to keep patients and clinicians safe
recommendations: Do not eliminate usability testing requirements
Maintain safeguards to ensure safety and usability
notable_quote: Please keep our patients and clinicians safe and keep usability testing in place!

summary: Patient ID Now opposes the removal of the §170.315(b)(1) Transitions of Care certification criterion because it is the only criterion referencing patient matching, which is critical for patient safety and privacy. They urge ONC to maintain this criterion until better patient matching standards or criteria are established.
specific_provisions: HR 2002 Patient Matching and Transparency in Certified Health IT Act of 2025
key_arguments: Removing the 'create' section of Transitions of Care removes the only certification criterion referencing patient matching.
Patient matching data in CCD improves patient safety by reducing misidentification and medical errors.
Current USCDI elements lack standardized data entry formats, causing interoperability and matching challenges.
Patient misidentification leads to significant patient safety risks, privacy breaches, and financial costs.
recommendations: Do not remove §170.315(b)(1) Transitions of Care certification criterion until alternative patient matching criteria are in place.
Adopt provisions from HR 2002 or MATCH IT Act to define a minimum data set and data standards for patient matching.
Update USCDI to facilitate better patient matching with standardized demographic data formats.
notable_quote: The inclusion of patient matching criteria within the continuity of care document improves patient safety and security.

summary: The Massachusetts Health Data Consortium supports the HTI-5 deregulatory efforts to modernize health IT certification and advance API-forward interoperability but emphasizes the need to prevent burden shifting, maintain security and interoperability floors, and provide clear implementation roadmaps. They urge ONC to address operational barriers, ensure trust, and avoid pay-to-play access to electronic health information.
key_arguments: Burden reduction must decrease total friction without shifting costs or risks downstream to providers, payers, or implementers.
Operational reliability and predictable, reusable integration are critical as interoperability evolves toward API-forward workflows.
Maintaining minimum, testable security expectations is essential to preserve trust during deregulation.
Clear implementation roadmaps and responsibility assignments are needed to reduce uncertainty and rework.
recommendations: Pair removed or revised regulations with clear crosswalks and responsibility assignments.
Preserve an interoperability floor during the transition to APIs.
Discourage network-by-network API arrangements; require scalable, reusable integration patterns.
Define minimum, testable API security baselines to avoid security gaps.
Provide an executable implementation roadmap with timelines and 'removed → replaced by' crosswalks.
Assign clear responsibility to developers for displaced capabilities and commit to post-final-rule evaluation.
Include worked examples for high-impact use cases.
Retain meaningful interoperability performance signals to maintain accountability.
Establish enforceable guardrails on fees and licensing to prevent pay-to-play EHI access.
Make information-blocking updates administrable with practical examples and checklists.
Ensure TEFCA remains effective without limiting adoption of superior API-based exchange.
notable_quote: Burden reduction will succeed only if it decreases total friction across the ecosystem - not if it shifts cost, risk, or bespoke integration work.

summary: The commenter, a medical malpractice attorney, strongly opposes the HTI-5 deregulatory proposals, emphasizing that removing audit log requirements will undermine the integrity and traceability of electronic medical records, which are critical for detecting falsification in legal cases. He urges ONC to establish a modern, enforceable audit trail standard before eliminating existing certification safeguards.
key_arguments: Federally required audit trails are essential to detect fabrication, backdating, and post-event alterations in EMRs.
Removing audit log requirements will hide falsified documentation and weaken accountability amid increasing automation and AI use.
Cost savings to developers do not account for harm to patients or increased forensic costs in litigation.
Modern EMR complexity demands enforceable audit standards before deregulation.
recommendations: Finalize and codify a modern audit trail standard that flags AI-generated entries and identifies copy-forward sources.
Require audit continuity across all platforms involved in patient care.
Ensure audit trails are non-privileged and fully accessible to patients.
Prohibit automatic destruction of care-related data and require retention in native, machine-readable formats.
Pause deregulatory removals until a comprehensive audit framework is implemented.
notable_quote: Efficiency cannot come at the expense of integrity.

summary: The commenter supports the proposed deregulatory actions, noting that standardization reduces errors during upgrades and improves data accuracy. They suggest that if more data is needed for research, special studies can be conducted instead of burdening routine data collection.
key_arguments: Standardization leads to more accurate data
Frequent edits during upgrades cause burden
Research can rely on special studies for additional data
recommendations: Implement standardization to reduce errors and burden
Allow research data needs to be met through special studies rather than routine data collection
notable_quote: If you make things standard you will get accurate data and if research need more data then u can have a special study done.

summary: Trinity Health appreciates efforts to reduce regulatory burden but strongly opposes many proposed deregulatory changes in HTI-5, citing risks to patient safety, data privacy, interoperability, and compliance with existing federal regulations. They urge ONC to maintain key certification criteria until FHIR adoption is widespread and comprehensive risk evaluations are completed.
specific_provisions: Privacy and Security Framework certification criteria (§170.315(d))
Public Health Certification criteria (Transmission to Cancer Registries, Antimicrobial Use and Resistance Reporting, eCR Reporting)
Information Blocking Infeasibility and Manner Exceptions
key_arguments: Removing demographic gender identity data conflicts with federal non-discrimination regulations and risks undermining patient trust.
Eliminating Care Coordination criteria risks fragmenting the EHR marketplace and harming quality and continuity of care.
Removing privacy and security certification criteria conflicts with HHS OCR security regulations and weakens protections against threats to electronic medical records.
Premature removal of public health reporting criteria threatens critical public health surveillance and response capabilities.
recommendations: Do not finalize changes to Patient Demographics criteria.
Maintain Care Coordination criteria to support patient safety and quality.
Retain Privacy and Security Framework certification criteria until comprehensive risk evaluation and HTI-6 finalization.
Maintain Patient Engagement Certification criteria to support federal program compliance.
Preserve Public Health reporting criteria for cancer registries, antimicrobial use, and eCR.
Do not finalize removal of Design and Performance Certification criteria.
Codify balanced requirements for automated access and use before finalizing related provisions.
Address misuse before changing the Infeasibility Exception in information blocking.
Delay finalizing Manner Exception changes until FHIR adoption is widespread.
Maintain TEFCA Manner Exception until governance and network maturity.
notable_quote: Removing demographic indicators for only one specific population may be perceived as disrespectful and could undermine our patients’ trust.

summary: The American Academy of Dermatology Association supports burden reduction efforts in the HTI-5 proposed rule but urges caution to avoid clinical care disruptions and patient safety risks. They emphasize retaining certain patient demographic data capture, AI transparency requirements, and call for clear transition plans and alignment with CMS reporting.
key_arguments: Removing sexual orientation, gender identity, and sex parameters risks clinical miscommunication and patient harm.
Eliminating C-CDA criteria before new standards are implemented may create informational gaps.
Removing AI transparency requirements undermines accountability and risks AI-related errors.
Deregulation should not create data or clinical care gaps and must align with CMS reporting requirements.
recommendations: Retain patient demographic data capture criteria and re-institute them in USCDI.
Develop guidance on capturing patient demographic data if criteria are removed.
Create a seamless transition plan before removing certification criteria to avoid care disruptions.
Reconsider removal of AI transparency requirements to maintain accountability.
Coordinate with CMS to align certification criteria with provider reporting requirements.
Prioritize physician education on information blocking rules and provide opportunities for corrective action before penalties.
notable_quote: AADA strongly urges ASTP/ONC to reconsider removal of transparency on algorithms and programming used in AI clinical decision-making models.

summary: Dartmouth Health supports the goal of accelerating API-driven interoperability and reducing duplicative burdens but expresses concern that several deregulatory actions in HTI-5 risk creating gaps that could slow interoperability and weaken necessary transparency and risk management, especially for predictive Decision Support Interventions (DSIs) and public health data exchange. They urge retention of minimum guardrails and standards to ensure patient safety, data consistency, and trustworthiness of medical records.
specific_provisions: 45 C.F.R. § 170.315(b)(11)(iv)–(vi) (predictive DSIs transparency and risk management)
health IT certification criteria related to data standards and export
key_arguments: Removing transparency and risk management elements for predictive DSIs risks patient harm and weakens clinical trust.
Minimum guardrails for DSIs can be maintained with low-burden, machine-readable Evidence Artifacts instead of prescriptive documentation.
Reducing or eliminating health IT standards will slow data flow and increase variability, harming public health data exchange and surveillance.
Lack of consistent standards forces public health agencies to handle multiple vendor-specific implementations, increasing inefficiency.
recommendations: Retain minimum guardrails for predictive DSIs including intended use, monitoring, auditability, and human override pathways.
Require certified Health IT Modules to support exportable, machine-readable Evidence Artifacts for predictive DSIs.
Maintain standards and certification requirements that ensure data is created, collected, and preserved in usable, searchable, and sharable formats before removing functional guardrails.
notable_quote: We are concerned that several deregulatory steps risk introducing architectural gaps that may slow real world interoperability.

summary: eHealth Exchange supports clarifications to Information Blocking definitions to include automated technologies and removal of certain exceptions but strongly opposes ONC's primary Manner Exception proposal due to overbroad contract definitions that threaten trusted health information networks. They recommend adopting ONC's alternative proposals to avoid harming HINs/HIEs and under-resourced organizations.
key_arguments: Clarifying 'access', 'use', and 'exchange' to explicitly include automated technologies avoids regulatory ambiguity.
Removing the Third Party Seeking Modification Use Condition supports innovation and provider access to third-party software.
The proposed 'contract of adhesion' definition is overbroad and would disrupt standardized agreements critical to trusted exchange.
The primary Manner Exception proposal would raise costs and create compliance crises for health information networks.
recommendations: Finalize clarifications to 'access', 'use', and 'exchange' definitions to include automated technologies.
Remove the Third Party Seeking Modification Use Condition from the Infeasibility Exception.
Retain 'similarly situated' language in the Manner Exception Exhausted Condition with suggested alternative wording.
Reject the primary Manner Exception proposal adding contractual limitations; instead, adopt the alternative proposal removing § 171.301(a)(2).
Remove the TEFCA Manner Exception consistent with changes to Exchange Purposes SOP.
notable_quote: The proposed “contract of adhesion” definition is substantially overbroad... and would create an immediate compliance crisis for HINs/HIEs.

summary: The Texas Health Informatics Alliance emphasizes the need for mandatory AI governance within certified health IT to ensure patient safety, equity, and quality. They urge alignment of HTI-5 with the ANSI 2800:2025 AI Governance Framework and call for explicit data quality and governance requirements to support effective AI oversight.
key_arguments: AI is embedded in healthcare workflows but often unreported and ungoverned, posing risks to patient safety and equity.
AI governance should be a shared responsibility across clinical, technical, and administrative domains.
Alignment with ANSI 2800:2025 provides a comprehensive, operational framework for AI governance.
Strong data governance including provenance, bias monitoring, and accountability is essential for effective AI oversight.
recommendations: Establish baseline expectations for AI governance in certified EHR technologies within HTI-5.
Encourage or require alignment with the ANSI 2800:2025 AI Governance Framework.
Include explicit expectations for data provenance, bias monitoring, ongoing model evaluation, and data integrity accountability.
notable_quote: AI governance must be treated as a patient safety, equity, quality, and leadership responsibility.

commenter_name: College of Healthcare Information Management Executives (CHIME)
organization: College of Healthcare Information Management Executives (CHIME)
summary: CHIME supports deregulatory actions that reduce burden on healthcare providers but urges ONC to ensure meaningful burden reduction without compromising patient safety, interoperability, or privacy. They recommend extended implementation timelines and retaining key privacy, security, and patient matching criteria until alternatives are established.
key_arguments: Deregulatory actions must result in measurable burden reduction for providers, not just shift burdens from vendors to providers.
Implementation timelines should be at least 24-36 months to accommodate vendor development, provider training, workforce shortages, and competing mandates.
Removing patient matching references in transitions of care criteria risks patient safety until alternative standards are adopted.
Regulatory changes should not weaken essential privacy, security, interoperability, or patient safety safeguards.
recommendations: Retain baseline privacy and security certification criteria under § 170.315(d) and associated framework under § 170.550(h).
Do not finalize removal of transitions of care criteria referencing patient matching until alternative standards are adopted.
Set implementation timelines no shorter than 24-36 months after final rule publication.
Consider cumulative regulatory burden on providers across federal programs when finalizing deregulatory actions.
notable_quote: Deregulation that merely shifts compliance burdens from technology vendors to providers will not achieve the Administration’s goals.

summary: No substantive comment text available (attachment only or blank).

Civitas Networks for Health supports modernization and deregulatory efforts focusing on FHIR-based APIs but urges caution in removing certification requirements for foundational standards like C-CDA and HL7 v2 messaging. They emphasize preserving interoperability, accountability, and operational reliability during transitions, and recommend clear guidance on information blocking exceptions to avoid penalizing good-faith actors.
key_arguments: Removing certification for foundational standards risks degradation of critical interoperability functions relied upon by providers and public health.
Certification reductions shift oversight burdens onto providers, states, and intermediaries who may lack capacity to validate health IT functionality.
Interoperability policy must balance modernization with maintaining baseline standards and real-world operational reliability.
Information blocking exception revisions should preserve flexibility for good-faith actors managing complex, heterogeneous exchange environments.
recommendations: Clearly articulate how essential functions formerly in certification will be preserved or overseen via alternative mechanisms.
Maintain enforcement of both FHIR APIs and baseline exchange standards during defined transition periods with transparent milestones.
Emphasize real-world interoperability testing including inter-version compatibility and practical exchange performance.
Provide clear examples and guidance illustrating permissible good-faith alternative manners of exchange under information blocking exceptions.
Affirm that reasonable sequencing, transition periods, and accommodation of recipient limitations are not information blocking.
notable_quote: Modernization must account for the reality that a significant portion of exchange today remains dependent on widely implemented standards.

summary: WEDI supports the overall goal of reducing health IT certification burdens but raises concerns about removing key certification criteria too quickly, especially those related to clinical decision support, C-CDA, and privacy/security. They emphasize the need for a cautious transition to ensure patient care and data security are not compromised.
key_arguments: Removing certification criteria may lead developers to drop functionalities users depend on, especially for new market entrants.
The transition from C-CDA to FHIR-based exchange is premature and risks disrupting clinical document exchange and patient care.
Privacy and security controls are critical and should not be weakened given ongoing cyber threats; embedding these in products is essential.
Compressed timelines for deregulatory changes do not allow adequate time for developers and users to adapt.
recommendations: Remove Patient Demographics certification criterion and replace with USCDI v3 elements.
Do not finalize the proposed revisions to Clinical Decision Support certification criteria.
Retain the C-CDA creation and receipt certification criteria until further evaluation of FHIR-based alternatives is completed.
Maintain current privacy and security certification criteria to ensure robust protections and avoid shifting burdens to providers.
notable_quote: We do not believe this is an appropriate time to remove any privacy and security controls or protections for patient health information.

summary: MEDITECH supports the HTI-5 deregulatory actions aimed at reducing duplicative certification requirements and aligning standards to reduce burden. They emphasize the need for clear timelines for USCDI updates, caution about removing C-CDA sending requirements without transitional safeguards, and advocate for balanced revisions rather than full eliminations in areas like decision support and audit logs.
specific_provisions: § 170.315(e)(1) View, download, and transmit to 3rd party
key_arguments: Removing or revising certification criteria reduces developer and provider burden and aligns with real-world usage.
Adequate lead time (minimum 24 months) is essential for USCDI version updates to ensure safe and reliable implementation.
Complete removal of some requirements (e.g., C-CDA sending) risks disrupting existing workflows without clear transitional guidance.
A risk-tiered, harmonized federal framework for AI-related transparency and risk management is preferable to burdensome, inconsistent requirements.
recommendations: Provide at least 24 months lead time for USCDI version changes to allow safe development and deployment.
Consider removing §170.315(a)(5) Patient Demographics entirely and defer to USCDI demographic data class.
Retain C-CDA sending requirements in §170.315(b)(1) during transition and consider removing sending from §170.315(e)(1) due to low patient use.
Revise rather than eliminate decision support source attribute and risk management requirements to support transparency and a risk-tiered approach.
Retain audit log related certification criteria (§170.315(d)(2), (d)(3), (d)(10)) to ensure HIPAA compliance and provide guidance to developers.
Clarify CMS’s plans before removing automated numerator recording and measure calculation criteria to avoid measurement inconsistencies.
notable_quote: Greater consistency across these frameworks is essential to minimizing confusion, implementation burden, and unnecessary cost.

summary: The commenter supports HTI-5's goals to reduce burden and modernize standards but opposes removing the §170.315(f)(4) certification criterion for cancer registry transmission. They argue that FHIR-based resources are immature, removal risks disrupting timely cancer reporting, and eliminating certification incentives may reduce interoperability and data quality.
key_arguments: FHIR-based resources for cancer registry reporting are not mature or widely implemented.
Removing the certification criterion risks disrupting timely, standardized cancer incidence reporting.
Eliminating certification incentives may reduce EHR support for interoperability and increase burden on jurisdictions.
Cancer reporting is critical for public health monitoring, research, and prevention strategies.
recommendations: Retain §170.315(f)(4) transmission to cancer registries certification criterion until FHIR-based reporting matures.
Implement a phased transition plan with defined minimum content expectations and coordinated testing to prevent reporting gaps.
notable_quote: Removal of §170.315(f)(4) risks disrupting timely incidence cancer reporting and increases provider burden.

commenter_name: Cooperative Exchange, The National Clearinghouse Association
organization: Cooperative Exchange, The National Clearinghouse Association
summary: The Cooperative Exchange supports modernization and burden reduction but warns that removing many certification criteria risks interoperability fragmentation, shifts burdens to providers and intermediaries, and weakens cybersecurity. They urge ONC to maintain baseline expectations, clarify AI accountability, align information blocking rules with operational control, and recognize the hybrid interoperability environment.
key_arguments: Removing certification criteria does not eliminate operational needs but shifts burden to providers and intermediaries, increasing variability and costs.
AI-driven automated access requires clear accountability assigned to AI deployers, not neutral intermediaries.
Narrowing information blocking exceptions risks misallocating liability to intermediaries who lack discretionary control over data release.
The healthcare ecosystem is hybrid; a FHIR-only approach is insufficient and transition must be phased to avoid disruption.
recommendations: Retain or clearly articulate minimum baseline interoperability and security expectations despite certification removals.
Publish impact analyses on removed criteria and establish post-final monitoring for unintended consequences.
Assign AI access responsibility to entities deploying AI and clarify auditability and traceability requirements.
Distinguish intermediaries from decision-makers in information blocking rules and align enforcement with operational control.
Recognize hybrid interoperability realities and engage clearinghouses in implementation planning.
Maintain baseline cybersecurity expectations referencing recognized frameworks and coordinate across federal agencies.
notable_quote: Deregulation should not unintentionally fragment baseline interoperability expectations or weaken cybersecurity and trust.

summary: The National Rural Health Association expresses concern that the HTI-5 proposed deregulatory actions may disproportionately harm rural health providers due to their limited resources, infrastructure, and cybersecurity capacity. They urge ONC to carefully evaluate the impact on rural hospitals before removing certification criteria that serve as essential safeguards.
key_arguments: High certified EHR adoption rates do not reflect rural providers' limited operational and cybersecurity capacity.
Removing certification criteria risks weakening baseline protections critical for rural hospitals with constrained resources.
Rural providers face persistent digital divides, including limited broadband and workforce shortages, impairing interoperability and security.
HTI-5 deregulatory actions may widen disparities between urban and rural health systems and threaten rural health infrastructure sustainability.
recommendations: Conduct and publish a rural-stratified impact analysis before finalizing removals of certification criteria.
Maintain certification guardrails that serve as minimum enforceable floors for cybersecurity and interoperability in rural settings.
Consider the cumulative impact of deregulatory changes on rural cybersecurity posture and vendor accountability.
Ensure federal alignment supports state rural health transformation initiatives dependent on stable certification frameworks.
notable_quote: Certification standards are not redundant administrative requirements; they often serve as the minimum enforceable floor ensuring basic protections.

summary: DirectTrust supports the overall goals of reducing regulatory burden and modernizing certification but urges retention of certification criteria supporting Direct Secure Messaging and privacy/security standards. They emphasize that removing these could increase operational burdens and disrupt critical, widely used document-based exchange workflows that are not yet replaced by FHIR push models.
specific_provisions: § 170.202(a)(2) Direct Standard and Applicability Statement for Secure Health Transport
key_arguments: Removing certification criteria may shift burden to other stakeholders and increase costs due to variability and custom integrations.
Direct Secure Messaging remains the only nationally deployed, standards-based push transport for cross-organizational referrals and transitions of care.
Certification ensures consistent implementation and interoperability at scale, which is critical for many clinical and public health workflows.
FHIR push messaging is not yet operational at scale to replace document-based exchange supported by Direct Secure Messaging.
recommendations: Retain certification criteria supporting Direct Secure Messaging and related standards to preserve reliable, consistent document-based exchange.
Maintain stand-alone privacy and security certification criteria to ensure patient data protection, especially in rural and under-resourced settings.
Align related certification criteria to ensure consistent implementation of Direct interfaces.
Support modernization efforts without disrupting existing operational workflows that function at scale.
notable_quote: Direct Secure Messaging remains the only nationally deployed, standards-based push exchange mechanism.

summary: The commenter supports modernization and moving toward FHIR-based interoperability while emphasizing that deregulation should not compromise patient safety, data accuracy, or privacy protections. They advocate for maintaining core technical safeguards such as audit logs, authentication, and encryption despite reducing outdated requirements.
specific_provisions: removal of Consolidated CDA (C-CDA) creation certification criterion
removal or reduction of audit log requirements
removal of privacy and security certification requirements including authentication, encryption, audit logging
key_arguments: Removing outdated technical requirements is beneficial to reduce burden and focus on modern standards.
Core functions supporting safe clinical decision-making must be preserved to avoid risks like delayed care or errors.
Security safeguards such as audit logs, authentication, and encryption are foundational to patient privacy and trust.
Public health reporting simplification must not compromise reliability and completeness essential for safety.
recommendations: Preserve core technical safeguards that support patient safety and accurate data exchange.
Maintain consistent baseline security functions in certified systems despite reducing duplication.
Ensure clarity and stability in USCDI data element updates to avoid confusion or gaps.
Balance expanded automated and AI data access with guardrails to protect system integrity and patient safety.
Simplify public health reporting only if reliability and completeness are preserved.
notable_quote: Modernization and the move toward more flexible, FHIR-based interoperability is supported. At the same time, patient safety must remain the guiding principle.

summary: The American Immunization Registry Association (AIRA) expresses concerns about removing interoperability and reporting requirements, emphasizing the need to maintain existing standards like HL7 V2 and CDA during the transition to FHIR. They urge retention of security and public health reporting requirements to ensure data protection and effective public health surveillance.
specific_provisions: Insights Conditions related to IIS and EHR interoperability
Certification Criteria for Health IT (security and authentication)
§ 171.102 Information Blocking definitions (including autonomous AI systems)
Removal of C-CDA creation certification criterion
key_arguments: Maintaining existing messaging standards (HL7 V2, CDA) is critical due to low FHIR adoption and implementation challenges.
Removing Insights Conditions will reduce transparency and interoperability between IIS and EHRs, harming clinical care and public health.
Security and privacy protections must be retained to protect patient data from unauthorized access.
Removing cancer registry reporting requirements without supporting FHIR implementation risks undermining critical public health surveillance.
recommendations: Retain and enforce existing Insights Conditions related to public health interoperability.
Maintain current security and authentication certification requirements until better privacy/security standards are developed.
Clarify Information Blocking definitions to ensure only authorized autonomous AI systems can access data.
Retain cancer registry reporting certification requirements until FHIR-based reporting is fully implemented and standardized.
notable_quote: We recommend the retention and enforcement of the existing Insights Conditions related to public health interoperability.

summary: The Alliance, representing patients and caregivers, expresses strong concern about removing transparency and safety requirements in the HTI-5 proposed rule. Members prioritize patient safety and algorithmic transparency over deregulation and worry that shifting evaluation burdens to hospitals will reduce confidence and exacerbate health inequities.
specific_provisions: shift of AI evaluation responsibility from developers to hospitals
key_arguments: Majority of members are concerned about AI-caused medical errors due to bias, hallucinations, and lack of interpretability.
Removing developer transparency shifts evaluation burden to hospitals lacking technical expertise and resources.
This shift risks exacerbating health inequities between large academic and smaller rural hospitals.
Collaborative evaluation between developers and hospitals is preferred to ensure safety and accuracy.
recommendations: Abandon the proposed rollback of mandatory transparency disclosures.
If rollbacks proceed, invest substantially in public communication about benefits to end users.
notable_quote: AI developers AND hospitals should jointly do AI system evaluation. More evaluation is better.

summary: The Anesthesia Patient Safety Foundation strongly opposes the removal of key health IT certification criteria proposed in HTI-5, emphasizing that these safety-related standards were established in response to real patient harm and remain essential. They argue that deregulation risks eroding critical safety protections, particularly in high-risk clinical environments, and that interoperability does not replace intrinsic product safety.
key_arguments: Certification criteria were implemented in direct response to documented patient harm and are essential safety interventions.
Widespread adoption of safety features is due to certification requirements, not a reason to remove them.
Security controls such as authentication and audit logs are critical safety measures, not just privacy protections.
Removing these criteria would weaken enforcement mechanisms and risk creating a two-tier market with uneven safety standards.
recommendations: Retain Safety-Enhanced Design requirement (170.315(g)(3)) in full.
Retain Quality Management System requirement (170.315(g)(4)).
Retain audit log and tamper-resistance requirements (170.315(d)(2), (d)(3), (d)(10)).
Retain authentication and multi-factor access control requirements (170.315(d)(1), (d)(13)).
Retain CDS source attribution and funding transparency requirements (170.315(b)(11)).
Reject the 'widespread adoption' rationale for removing safety requirements.
Clearly distinguish between interoperability validation and intrinsic product safety.
Preserve enforcement mechanisms that protect patients and public resources.
notable_quote: Removing core safety criteria shifts verification burdens onto organizations least equipped to perform independent evaluation.

summary: The commenter emphasizes the critical importance of maintaining accurate, complete, and trustworthy electronic medical records, particularly highlighting the need for modernized audit trail standards. They oppose the proposed deregulatory changes in HTI-5 that reduce certification requirements before establishing robust data creation, preservation, and audit standards to ensure patient safety and trust.
key_arguments: Current audit trail standards (ASTM E2147-18) are outdated and insufficient for modern clinical and technological environments.
Audit trails are essential for transparency, accountability, fraud prevention, billing integrity, and legal accountability.
Patient access to complete EMRs remains inadequate despite legal requirements.
Data must be durable and preserved, prohibiting ephemeral messaging or automatic data destruction to support ongoing care and compliance.
recommendations: Pause proposed certification changes until a modern, robust audit and preservation standard is finalized.
Develop a new audit trail standard that includes AI, medical device integration, granular metadata, fraud prevention, and detailed activity logging.
Prohibit ephemeral messaging and automatic destruction of health data to ensure data durability and compliance.
notable_quote: Audit trails are foundational—not optional—to billing integrity, patient trust, and legal accountability.

summary: The Texas Medical Association opposes many of the proposed deregulatory actions in HTI-5, arguing that removing key certification criteria risks patient safety, interoperability, and consistent quality of care. They urge ONC to amend rather than remove criteria to reduce burden while maintaining essential standards.
specific_provisions: 170.315(f)(5) Transmission to public health agencies – Electronic case reporting
170.315(f)(6) Transmission to public health agencies – Antimicrobial use and resistance reporting
170.315(f)(7) Transmission to public health agencies – Health care surveys
key_arguments: Removing certification criteria risks fragmentation of EHR market and loss of baseline safety, usability, and interoperability.
Certification provides a durable accountability framework critical for meeting federal program requirements like MIPS.
Eliminating criteria such as clinical information reconciliation and decision support transparency compromises patient safety.
Transitioning fully to FHIR standards is premature; legacy standards like C-CDA remain essential for care coordination.
recommendations: Retain and update clinical decision support criteria to reflect AI and machine learning advances rather than removing them.
Maintain family health history certification to preserve vital structured data for continuity of care.
Do not eliminate care coordination and reconciliation criteria to ensure reliable data exchange and patient safety.
Preserve design and performance criteria to maintain measurement reliability and reduce risk of misreporting.
Retain safety-enhanced design criteria to ensure usability and patient safety are prioritized.
Keep consolidated CDA creation performance criteria until FHIR standards are ubiquitously adopted.
Retain public health certification criteria to avoid reporting gaps during transition to FHIR-based reporting.
notable_quote: Removing certification requirements risks a fragmented EHR market with variable safety, usability, and exchange capabilities.

summary: AMIA supports the goals of reducing burden and improving interoperability but considers the HTI-5 deregulatory actions premature and rushed. They caution that removing many certification criteria, including AI transparency requirements and C-CDA, risks reducing equitable access, patient safety, and interoperability progress.
specific_provisions: Decision Support Interventions (DSI) AI model card requirements
key_arguments: Removing many certification criteria risks unbundling features into costly add-ons, limiting access for smaller providers.
Eliminating AI transparency requirements prematurely removes emerging standards before sufficient implementation data exists.
Narrowing reporting metrics to only FHIR use risks losing visibility into interoperability gaps across diverse care settings.
Transitioning away from widely adopted standards like C-CDA and HL7 v2.5.1 without mature alternatives is rushed and burdensome.
recommendations: Assess potential equity impacts of removing certification criteria and clarify protections for patient access and interoperability.
Retain AI transparency requirements like model cards until sufficient data supports their removal.
Maintain broad, outcome-oriented interoperability metrics across all care settings, not just FHIR adoption.
Shift certification focus toward standardized data models and APIs rather than broad EHR functionality.
